file_name: Karki et al. - 2025 - Deep learning for early skin cancer detection Combining segmentation, augmentation, and transfer le.json

did_this_paper_use_ai_to_obtain_results_.details: The paper used deep learning techniques, specifically a U-Net segmentation model and a ResNet-50 backbone with transfer learning for a feedforward classification network, to classify skin lesion images as benign or malignant using the ISIC 2016 and Kaggle datasets.

is_this_paper_a_review_paper_or_a_resear.details: The paper presents original experimental work where the authors developed and evaluated a deep learning-based classification model using the ISIC 2016 and Kaggle datasets. It details specific methods (U-Net segmentation, ResNet-50 transfer learning), experimental design (epochs, batch sizes, pre-processing), and reports new quantitative results (accuracy 92.80%, precision 98.64%, recall 86.80%). Although it includes a literature review section, its primary purpose is to report new technical findings and model performance, not to summarize prior literature.

title: Deep Learning for Early Skin Cancer Detection: Combining Segmentation, Augmentation, and Transfer Learning
brief_summary: This study developed a deep learning model to classify skin lesion images as benign or malignant for early skin cancer detection. The authors utilized the ISIC 2016 dataset to train a U-Net segmentation model and the Kaggle dataset of 10,000 images for classification. The methodology combined U-Net segmentation with Canny edge detection to highlight lesion boundaries, which were then fed into a ResNet-50 backbone classifier using transfer learning. The model was evaluated using accuracy, precision, recall, and F1-score on a test set of 1,000 images. The proposed approach achieved an accuracy of 92.80%, a precision of 98.64%, and a recall of 86.80%. The results indicate that integrating segmentation with transfer learning improves classification performance compared to previous studies on similar datasets.

lesion_types_studied.details: Used as one of the two binary classification targets in the Kaggle dataset (5000 training images, 500 test images).

all_task_types.target: Binary diagnostic category (benign vs. malignant skin lesions)
all_task_types.details: The paper explicitly states the development of a 'deep learning-based classification model to classify the skin lesion images as benign (non-cancerous) and malignant (cancerous).' The model uses a ResNet-50 backbone with transfer learning for a feedforward network to predict discrete classes. Performance was evaluated using accuracy (92.80%), precision (98.64%), and recall (86.80%).

all_task_types.target: Binary segmentation mask highlighting the lesion region
all_task_types.details: A U-Net model was trained on the ISIC 2016 dataset to generate binary segmentation masks. These masks were used to highlight lesion boundaries via Canny edge detection as a pre-processing step for the classification model. The segmentation task achieved an F1 score of 0.8965 and an Intersection over Union score of 0.7966.

datasets_used.name: ISIC 2016
datasets_used.description: A public dataset from the International Skin Imaging Collaboration (ISIC) used for training the U-Net segmentation model. Images are in JPG format with ground truth masks in PNG format (1022 × 767 pixels resolution).
classes_used: Lesion segmentation mask (binary: lesion vs. skin)

use.training: Used to train the U-Net segmentation model (900 images).
use.testing: Used to test the segmentation model (379 images).

datasets_used.name: Kaggle Melanoma Skin Cancer Dataset
datasets_used.description: A dataset of 10,000 skin lesion images collected from different ISIC directories, hosted on Kaggle. Images are in JPG format with a resolution of 300 × 300 pixels. Used for the binary classification task (benign vs. malignant).

use.training: Used to train the classification model (ResNet-50 backbone) with 80% of the data (9605 images).
use.validation: Used for validation during the training process (20% of the data, 1995 images).
use.testing: Used for final testing of the classification model (1000 images).

hypothesis_testing.interpretation: The paper did not report any formal hypothesis testing, p-values, or significance tests. Performance was evaluated using descriptive metrics (accuracy, precision, recall, F1-score, ROC-AUC) and compared qualitatively with previous studies.

ISIC 2016 Dataset.notes: The segmentation model was trained on 900 images and tested on 379 images. The result suggests the model is capable of differentiating lesion boundaries with minimal misclassification.

Kaggle Melanoma Skin Cancer Dataset.notes: The model was tested on 1000 images (500 benign, 500 malignant). The high precision indicates accurate classification of benign images, while the lower recall indicates room for improvement in detecting malignant cases. The model showed the best score in accuracy, precision, and F1 by a small margin compared to previous studies listed in Table 1.

architecture_used.main_model: U-Net (for segmentation) and ResNet-50 with a custom feedforward neural network (for classification)
architecture_used.pretraining: ResNet-50 pre-trained model used as a feature extractor; layers were initially frozen then unfrozen for fine-tuning.
architecture_used.input_size: 224 × 224 pixels (for classification model); 1022 × 767 pixels (original ISIC 2016 images for segmentation)

segmentation_model.name: U-Net
segmentation_model.encoder: Five down-sampling blocks; each block has two convolutional layers (kernel size 3x3, padding 1) followed by ReLU activation; feature channels double from 64 to 1024
segmentation_model.decoder: Deconvolution layers to up-sample feature maps; feature channels reduced from 512 to 64; final 1x1 convolution layer reduces 64 channels to 1 channel for binary segmentation

classification_model.head: Multi-layer fully connected architecture (feedforward neural network) with 5 layers
classification_model.regularization: Dropout layer with probability 0.4 after each ReLU activation

classification_training.dataset: Kaggle Melanoma Skin Cancer Dataset (9605 training images)

preprocessing_techniques: Color jittering to simulate different lighting conditions
Normalization using ImageNet mean [0.485, 0.456, 0.406] and standard deviation [0.229, 0.224, 0.225] for RGB channels
U-Net-based segmentation to isolate lesion regions
Canny edge detection to delineate lesion boundaries from segmented images
Overlaying segmentation edges onto pre-processed images to highlight the lesion region

data_augmentation_techniques.details: Applied with a probability of 0.5 to simulate different orientations of the lesions.
Random rotations of up to 30 degrees were applied to desensitize the model to the orientations of lesion areas.
Resized crop with a crop factor of 0.8 in the height.
Applied to simulate different lighting conditions.
Applied as part of the pre-processing and augmentation steps.

conclusions: Integrating deep learning techniques with proper data pre-processing, including segmentation and augmentation, improves the model's performance for skin cancer detection.

Using U-Net segmentation combined with Canny edge detection to highlight lesion boundaries effectively isolates the region of interest, which improves the classifier's ability to distinguish between benign and malignant lesions.

The proposed model, which combines U-Net segmentation with a ResNet-50 backbone using transfer learning, achieved an accuracy of 92.80%, a precision of 98.64%, and a recall of 86.80% on the Kaggle dataset.

The integration of segmentation results into the classification pipeline allows the model to focus more on the lesion area, thereby enhancing classification accuracy compared to previous studies on similar datasets.
While the model demonstrated high accuracy and precision, the recall for malignant cases (86.80%) indicates room for improvement in detecting harder cancerous lesions.

Generalization across different image conditions, skin tones, and noise remains a major challenge in deep learning for skin cancer detection, necessitating further work on expanding datasets and testing additional architectures.
important_remarks: The study relies on the Kaggle Melanoma Skin Cancer Dataset, which aggregates images from various ISIC directories; this may introduce dataset bias and lacks the rigorous annotation standards of dedicated clinical trials.

The classification task is binary (benign vs. malignant), which oversimplifies clinical reality by grouping diverse malignancies (e.g., melanoma, basal cell carcinoma, squamous cell carcinoma) into a single class, potentially masking performance disparities among specific cancer types.

The model achieved high precision (98.64%) but lower recall (86.80%), indicating a significant number of false negatives (69 out of 500 malignant cases); in a clinical screening context, this high false-negative rate is a critical safety concern.

The paper does not report formal statistical hypothesis testing, p-values, or confidence intervals, limiting the ability to assess the statistical significance of performance improvements over baseline models.

External validation is limited; the model was tested on the same Kaggle dataset used for training (with an 80/20 split), raising concerns about data leakage or overfitting to the specific distribution of that dataset rather than generalizing to unseen clinical populations.

The segmentation model was trained on ISIC 2016 and applied to the Kaggle dataset; differences in image resolution (1022x767 vs 300x300) and acquisition protocols between these datasets may affect the robustness of the segmentation-to-classification pipeline.

Comparison with prior works is limited by inconsistent reporting; Table 1 shows that several comparative studies did not report precision, recall, or F1-scores, making a comprehensive performance benchmark difficult.

The authors acknowledge that generalization across different skin tones, image conditions, and noise remains a major challenge, suggesting the current results may not be representative of diverse global populations.
